nodes.name: Greg Mitchell
nodes.description: Survived the Alcatel restructuring, promoted in 2003. Manages a team now. More stable but works longer hours. Shows pride through nods and handshakes, not words.

nodes.name: Karen Mitchell
nodes.description: Real estate career is real now — she's good at it. Megan's high school years are consuming. Worries less about Drew because he seems so steady. Doesn't realize that steadiness has a cost.

nodes.name: Megan Mitchell
nodes.description: 14, freshman at Plano Senior High. Varsity soccer. Pulling away from the family — closed door, phone calls, eye-rolling. Drew admires her and is invisible to her in roughly equal measure.

nodes.name: Carol Novak
nodes.description: The most important relationship in Drew's intellectual life. Taught him to garden. They fixed her back porch railing together. Gives him books without comment and lets him talk about them when he's ready.

nodes.name: Connor Huang
nodes.description: Getting into skateboarding and video games. Starting to care about what other kids think in ways Drew doesn't yet. The friendship is solid but the gap between their interests is widening.

nodes.name: Kyle Briggs
nodes.description: Fifth grade alpha. Bigger than everyone, dad coaches youth football. Tested Drew twice — lunch tray, called him a nerd. Drew didn't flinch either time, which confused Kyle enough to move on.

edges.visibility: high — Drew sees Greg every evening, Greg observes Drew's competence around the house directly
edges.information_flow: indirect — Greg learns about Drew's school life and social world through Karen, not through Drew. Direct channel limited to shared tasks and household projects.

edges.visibility: high — Karen is the primary household parent, sees Drew daily, checks homework, manages his schedule
edges.information_flow: direct — Karen observes Drew's daily life and receives reports from school. Drew filters what he shows her, defaulting to 'I'm fine' to avoid triggering her anxiety.

edges.visibility: low — Megan is absorbed in her own adolescence, barely registers Drew's daily life
edges.information_flow: minimal — they share a house but not information. Drew observes Megan; Megan does not observe Drew.

edges.visibility: high — Carol sees Drew several afternoons a week, in her home, in unstructured time where he is most himself
edges.information_flow: direct and unfiltered — Drew tells Carol things he doesn't tell his parents. Carol listens without redirecting. This is Drew's most honest communication channel.

edges.visibility: moderate — they see each other at school daily, but spend less time together outside school than they used to
edges.information_flow: direct but narrowing — they talk about surface things. Neither shares what's actually changing between them.

edges.visibility: moderate — they share a grade and a cafeteria. Kyle is aware of Drew as someone who didn't flinch. Drew is aware of Kyle as a weather pattern.
edges.information_flow: ambient — no direct communication, but fifth-grade social dynamics mean actions by either boy are observed by the peer group.

gatekeepers.gates: social access — who Drew can visit, where he can go, what activities he's allowed to do, how late he can stay out
gatekeepers.conditions: academic performance, good behavior, church attendance, being low-maintenance
gatekeepers.current_stance: permissive — Drew is the easy child. He doesn't ask for much, doesn't cause trouble, keeps his grades up. Karen grants access readily because he hasn't given her a reason not to.

gatekeepers.gates: autonomy and responsibility — what tasks Drew is trusted with, how much independence he gets in practical matters
gatekeepers.conditions: demonstrated competence, following through on tasks without being reminded
gatekeepers.current_stance: expanding — Greg has been steadily delegating more responsibility to Drew. Each completed task earns the next one. This is Greg's primary mode of expressing trust and pride.

rewarded.reward: trust, expanded autonomy, pride currency from Greg, relief from Karen's worry
delegation of responsibility (Greg), being left alone to think (Carol), not triggering anxiety (Karen)
social invisibility — the kind of invisibility that means adults don't worry about you
social belonging, normative approval. Drew has not engaged with this, and nobody has pushed hard because his other behaviors compensate.

punished.cost: anxiety spike in Karen, uncomfortable silence from Greg, social embarrassment. Drew has internalized this so thoroughly that punishment is rarely needed — he self-polices.
loss of autonomy, increased monitoring, Karen's worry shifting from background to foreground
loss of trust, which for Drew is loss of the currency he values most
exclusion, targeting by kids like Kyle. Drew navigates this by being unremarkable rather than by performing normalcy.
